Introduction I
Generative AI is moving fast, and that speed is a good thing in many industries. But in healthcare, where decisions can impact lives, speed without caution isn't innovation; it's risk.

This is a field where trust, nuance, and accountability matter. So, the question isn't just what Al can do, but what it should do, and how we ensure it strengthens, not strains, the systems we rely on.

This eBook brings together insights from people tackling that challenge head-on. They're not theorizing from the sidelines but building and deploying AI tools in real clinical settings. Their focus? Not hype, but impact. Not novelty, but meaningful, measurable progress.
We've structured this eBook around five key themes shaping the future of generative Al in healthcare:
1 Understanding where LLMs help and where they don't

In clinical care, requests aren't always literal, and emotions often matter as much as symptoms. You'll explore where large language models (LLMs) can support clinicians, like summarizing records or simplifying instructions, and where they fall short, like interpreting complex intent or emotional nuance.
2 Turning failure into fuel in pharma

Drug development is lengthy, expensive, and often unsuccessful. But failure doesn't have to be wasted. You'll see how Takeda uses Al to learn from every misstep, speeding up discovery, reducing risk, and making treatments more tailored and predictive.
3 Building trust through secure, compliant infrastructure

Al in healthcare can't be a black box. One chapter dives into how a secure LLMOps (large language model operations) framework helped turn an informal documentation tool into a scalable, compliant solution, freeing clinician time while meeting the strictest regulatory standards.
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
Introduction I
4 Designing AI for paediatrics, not just adapting it

Pediatric care has unique needs. At Great Ormond Street Hospital, teams build purpose-built, fine- tuned models that understand children's data and support overworked clinicians without cutting corners on oversight or ethics.
5 Scaling AI responsibly in a massive health system

Drug development is lengthy, expensive, and often unsuccessful. But failure doesn't have to be wasted. You'll see how Takeda uses Al to learn from every misstep, speeding up discovery, reducing risk, and making treatments more tailored and predictive.

aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
Key learnings

This eBook is about practical progress, not hypothetical breakthroughs. It's a snapshot of how Al is quietly reshaping healthcare, not by replacing humans, but by helping them work smarter, faster, and with greater focus.
You'll come away with:
A clearer view of where LLMs can drive real value in healthcare and where they can't.
Concrete lessons on designing AI tools that clinicians want to use.
Insights on governance, compliance, and the infrastructure needed to support safe deployment.
V Examples of how different healthcare organizations are putting generative AI to work, ethically, efficiently, and at scale.

Chapter 1 Opportunities & limitations of LLMs in healthcare
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
Opportunities & limitations of LLMs in healthcare

In healthcare, what a patient says is rarely the whole story, and that's exactly where artificial intelligence still falls short. Unlike consumer tech, where a request is often literal, clinical interactions are layered with fear, context, and unspoken concerns. When Al takes what's said at face value, it risks misunderstanding the nature of care.

That's the warning from Tamara Saver, who highlights how large language models (LLMs) often miss the "why behind the why." A teenager asking for a CT scan, or a woman seeking a back x-ray at 3 a.m., aren't just making medical requests; they're revealing more profound anxieties. A good doctor recognizes this and adjusts accordingly. Al, as it stands today, doesn't.

This chapter explores where LLMs can deliver value in healthcare and where they can't. When the question is straightforward and the data structured, AI can retrieve records, summarize instructions, and surface insights with impressive speed.

But when patient needs are emotional, complex, or evolving, Al falters. It can't interpret ambiguity. It can't empathize. And it certainly can't build the mental model a physician constructs instinctively.

Yet Saver is no AI skeptic. Her work at Vituity shows how the correct implementation makes all the difference. A sepsis prediction model embedded invisibly into lab panels led to earlier diagnoses and saved lives.

A reframed tool, pitched as an "Al Resident" rather than an all-knowing system, earned clinician trust and improved adoption. These examples prove successful healthcare Al isn't just about more innovative models. It's about seamless design, thoughtful deployment, and earning trust by acting as an assistant, not a replacement.

The future of Al in healthcare doesn't depend on hype or abstraction; it depends on precision, humility, and understanding where these tools truly fit. When AI supports genuine human care, not just digital efficiency, it can make medicine faster and more humane.
Opportunities & limitations of LLMs in healthcare
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
1 The problem: AI in healthcare needs clear intent

A lot of AI failures in healthcare happen because systems misread the intent behind what patients or clinicians say. Large language models often take requests at face value, but any doctor will tell you: the hard part is figuring out what's really going on beneath the surface.
Tamara Saver shares two examples that bring this to life:

In one, a 16-year-old shows up in the ER asking for a CT scan. His parents had plugged his symptoms into ChatGPT and were worried it was appendicitis. But after talking with the patient, the doctor realizes it's not about sharp pain: it's months of ongoing nausea. Totally different issue, totally different path forward. The family was chasing one answer, but the clinical reality pointed somewhere else.

In another, a woman comes in at 3 a.m. asking for a back x-ray. She's had pain for years. So why now? Turns out her brother was just diagnosed with cancer, and she's scared. She's not just looking for a scan, she's looking for reassurance.

These stories highlight what AI can miss: the deeper meaning behind a question or request. Good care depends on understanding a person's full story, history, fears, and context, not just what they say out loud. Tamara Saver puts it well: skilled clinicians are trained to spot fear, doubt, and what's left unsaid. LLMs, for now, just aren't built to pick up on that kind of emotional and situational depth.
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
2 Where LLMs work and where they fail in healthcare
Where LLMs excel
Saver points out a few places where large language models really can add value in clinical settings:
Searching structured medical data

When the question is straightforward and tied to existing records, LLMs can be a big help. They can quickly pull up a patient's cardiac history, current medications, or past imaging reports. Thanks to semantic search, doctors can spot patterns and find results that would usually take time and manual effort to dig through.
Creating patient-friendly communication

AI can help draft discharge notes in plain language that patients actually understand. Take a dancer with a dislocated shoulder, for example; rather than generic rehab advice, AI can tailor the message to encourage recovery in a way that aligns with dance goals, making it more personal and motivating.
Answering factual questions

Patient portals get many simple queries like, "When's my next tetanus shot?" These don't require deep thinking, just quick and accurate answers. LLMs can handle that well, pulling from existing records with speed and precision.
X Where LLMs fail
But there are serious limits to what they can do:
Understanding deeper intent

LLMs can't read between the lines or offer empathy. If someone asks, "Do I have cancer?" the model might give factual info, but it won't catch the fear or emotional weight behind the question. Saver says it clearly: understanding the real reason behind a question is critical in healthcare. Right now, Al can't ask follow-ups or sense what's left unsaid.
Building a full picture of the patient

Doctors don't just listen, they interpret. Based on how a patient responds, they might realize that back pain is stress-related, posture-related, or something worse. LLMs don't have that kind of flexibility. They can't adjust on the fly or connect the dots in the same way.
Handling unclear or incomplete info

Clinicians ask follow-ups to resolve gaps or inconsistencies. LLMs sometimes fill in the blanks with confident guesses, which can be dangerous. Hallucinations, made-up studies, or misread clinical details aren't just inconvenient; they can break trust and put patients at risk if not caught.
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
3 Case studies: successful AI implementation in healthcare
Saver highlights that even the best Al model won't succeed without smart implementation. Two real-world examples show how doing it right makes all the difference.
Predicting sepsis before it's too late

Sepsis is one of the top causes of death in hospitals. Most AI tools detect it after a patient is already in serious trouble. However, Vituity built a model that flags risk before symptoms show up, using trends in lab results.

What's smart is how they introduced it. The risk score is added to the normal lab report instead of triggering an alarm or making doctors click through a new dashboard. No extra steps, no new system to learn.

Doctors don't even realize they're using Al; they just see more useful lab data. This "invisible Al" setup helped them catch sepsis earlier, act faster, and save lives. Tamara Saver points out that adoption was seamless because it blended into existing workflows. No resistance, just results.
The "Al Resident" that earned doctors' trust
Getting doctors to trust AI is another hurdle, especially if it feels like a black box or comes off too sure of itself. But there's a clever workaround: speak their language.
Vituity called its system an "Al Resident", a digital version of a junior doctor. Instead of saying "Al recommends," the message says, "Your Al Resident suggests ... pending your review."

That small shift made a big impact. It felt familiar and collaborative, not bossy or rigid. Clinicians were more open to using it, which led to better uptake and better outcomes. As Saver explains, AI gains traction when it acts as backup, not a replacement for human judgment.
Step-by-step:
Test ordered
data analyzed
data analyzed
+ AI adds score
+ Doctor sees in normal report
Opportunities & limitations of LLMs in healthcare
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
4 Next steps and future vision

Saver's takeaway is simple: the future of Al in healthcare isn't about chasing buzzwords but getting it right. AI should support doctors, not try to take their place. That means careful integration, thorough testing, and using it only where it truly helps.

Innovation should be driven by hope, not frustration. If the industry keeps refining, keeps listening to what clinicians need, and stays focused on what actually works, AI has the potential to make healthcare both smarter and more compassionate.
"Al can be revolutionary, but only if we apply it where it actually works. Don't let the hype distract from real progress."
Opportunities & limitations of LLMs in healthcare
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
Key takeaways
Healthcare isn't a simple back-and-forth; it's layered and complex. For Al to be useful, it needs to understand more than just what's said on the surface.
V LLMs work best when things are clear and the data is clean. They're great for:
* Pulling info from medical records
* Answering straightforward questions
* Writing summaries that patients can actually understand
But they start to fall apart when things get messy, like when intent isn't clear or the data is incomplete. That's when they struggle with:
* Spotting subtle or complex diagnoses
* Picking up on fear, anxiety, or unspoken concerns
* Making sound decisions without human oversight
How AI is built into the system matters. It should be:
* Invisible when it can be, blending into existing workflows
* Framed in a familiar way, like an "Al Resident", to build trust
* Integrated into the tools doctors already use, not bolted on as an afterthought

Chapter 2 Fail faster, heal smarter: How AI is rewiring pharma
Fail faster, heal smarter: How AI is rewiring pharma
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook

Bringing a new drug from concept to market is notoriously slow and expensive. In fact, the process can often take over a decade and cost billions of dollars, with most attempts still ending in failure.
Despite the abundance of data generated through clinical trials, the pharmaceutical industry struggles to systematically capture and analyze these failures, frequently repeating past mistakes.

According to Leo Barella, Chief Technology Officer at Takeda, pharma companies have traditionally operated with the cautious precision of risk-averse organizations like NASA. This mindset, Barella argues, hampers innovation, slows progress, and prevents the industry from achieving exponential growth.

This chapter explores how Takeda is harnessing artificial intelligence to fundamentally rethink this model. Inspired by SpaceX's rapid iteration strategy, Barella advocates for an Al-driven approach that embraces failure as a critical source of learning.

By systematically analyzing data from unsuccessful trials and leveraging generative AI for early- stage drug discovery, Takeda is converting past setbacks into valuable insights, accelerating drug development, and improving patient outcomes.

Integrating AI-enabled real-time health monitoring and personalized medicine solutions promises to shift healthcare from reactive to proactive, significantly reducing misdiagnoses and adverse drug interactions.

The implications of this approach extend well beyond pharma. Barella's insights underscore a pivotal shift in how healthcare sectors might harness AI to not only speed up innovation but also enhance patient care and safety.

As AI-first thinking becomes mainstream, the pharmaceutical industry (and healthcare at large) stands poised on the brink of transformation. The question now is not if AI can reshape medicine, but how swiftly and effectively organizations can adapt to leverage these new capabilities.
Fail faster, heal smarter: How AI is rewiring pharma
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
1 The problem: Why pharma moves too slowly

Bringing a new drug to market takes time, often over a decade, and billions of dollars. And still, most drug candidates don't make it. Pharma is an industry built on failure, yet it rarely learns from those failures meaningfully and systematically.

One of the biggest roadblocks is poor data infrastructure. Clinical trials produce tons of valuable information, but much of it ends up siloed, ignored, or lost. When a trial fails, the lessons often vanish; no insights, improvements, just repeated mistakes.

As Barella puts it, pharma tends to operate like NASA: highly controlled, risk-averse, and slow to move. In 2023, a single faulty gasket delayed the Artemis mission. In drug development, small issues often lead to cautious decisions that drag the process out even more.

We still lean heavily on human judgment and one-off diagnostics, like annual checkups or single lab results. It's a reactive model, and it's slow. Failed trials and rejected drugs often aren't mined for insights, so we miss the chance to get better.
Barella sums it up:
"We manage science when we should be engineering it."
Until pharma adopts a mindset of fast iteration and continuous learning, it'll keep moving at a crawl, missing out on the real potential for rapid progress.
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
2 The solution: Using AI to learn from failure

Takeda is investing in AI to reshape how pharma handles failure, turning setbacks into smart, data-driven learning moments. The focus is on using AI to analyze why things go wrong and ensure those lessons drive progress.
What pharma can learn from SpaceX
Take a look at two very different approaches to space exploration: NASA vs. Spacex.

NASA has a long track record of success, but operates with a zero-failure mindset. Everything is planned down to the last detail, and mistakes are avoided. In 2023, a single faulty gasket stopped the Artemis rocket rollout, proving how risk-averse the culture is.
Spacex, on the other hand, leans into failure. Their rockets blow up during tests, and that's the point. Every crash is packed with data. Each test, even the failed ones, moves them forward faster.

Pharma needs to think more like SpaceX. A failed clinical trial or rejected drug shouldn't be a dead end. It should be a rich source of insight. With AI, companies can track every part of the process, analyze what happened, and use that knowledge to make smarter choices next time.
Failure isn't the enemy; it's fuel for faster innovation.
"Pharma must think more like SpaceX: fail fast, learn faster, and improve continuously."
AI-powered drug discovery

Generative AI is changing how new drugs are discovered. These models can simulate how molecules interact with diseases, speeding up the process of identifying promising candidates in the early stages.
AI also helps teams hold onto hard-earned knowledge. Even if a scientist leaves, the insights they've contributed don't walk out the door; Al systems keep that institutional memory intact.
And with access to massive datasets, from genomics and metabolomics to clinical records, AI can spot hidden patterns and connections that would've been impossible to find manually.
"We are an industry built on failure, but we lack a systematic way to remember and learn from it. Al can change that."
Fail faster, heal smarter: How AI is rewiring pharma
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
3 AI-powered personalized medicine

Most drugs today are designed for the average patient, not the individual. It's efficient, but it often means treatments don't work as well as they could. And drug interactions are still one of the most overlooked causes of serious side effects, leading to thousands of preventable deaths every year.

AI is shifting that approach. Instead of one-size-fits-all, it enables true personalization. Algorithms can predict how someone will respond to a drug based on their unique biology, medical history, and even lifestyle.
AI can also flag potential drug interactions before they become dangerous, giving doctors and patients a heads-up.
It's even possible to create "batch of one" medications, tailored to a person's specific genetic and biochemical makeup.
And with connected sensors tracking real-time data, early signs of side effects can be caught and addressed quickly, before they spiral into something worse.
The result? Less trial-and-error, fewer side effects, and way better outcomes.
"Drug interaction fatalities in the U.S. are equivalent to a Boeing 747 crashing every single day. Al can help stop this."
Fail faster, heal smarter: How AI is rewiring pharma
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
4 Transforming pharma with AI-first thinking
Takeda is reimagining how pharma works by weaving AI into every step of the process, from discovery to delivery.
Drug discovery. Generative AI helps design and refine new molecules before anything even hits the lab, saving time and money upfront.
Clinical trials. AI picks the right patient groups, predicts how things might play out, and adjusts trials on the fly to stay on track.
/ Diagnostics. Instead of relying on outdated one-time tests, Al-powered wearables and real- time monitoring give a continuous, more accurate view of patient health.
Personalized treatment. Algorithms fine-tune drug formulas and dosages to match each patient's biology, boosting safety and effectiveness.
Regulatory compliance. Smart automation handles paperwork, keeps up with changing rules, and helps speed up the approval process.

By putting AI at the core of everything, Takeda and the broader industry can stop moving in slow, cautious steps and start making real leaps. An AI-first mindset means faster decisions, better patient results, and a healthcare system that keeps getting smarter over time.
Fail faster, heal smarter: How AI is rewiring pharma
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
5 The problem: Why pharma moves too slowly
"We aren't competing against other pharma companies. We are competing against disease, and Al will be our most powerful weapon in that fight."

With AI, real-time diagnostics, and personalized medicine all coming together, the chance to truly transform patient care has never been bigger. By learning from failure, using smart automation, and focusing on individualized insights, pharma can shift from a reactive model to one that's proactive, precise, and genuinely patient-first.
Takeda is already on this path. The company is using AI to analyze failures across its research teams, turning every missed opportunity into a lesson that fuels future breakthroughs.
Generative AI is helping develop more targeted treatments faster, while wearables and AI-powered diagnostics are making real-time, always-on health monitoring a reality.

At the same time, Takeda is teaming up with top AI and health tech partners to help shape the future of medicine. These cross-industry collaborations are key to solving tough problems and making sure innovation actually improves lives.
Barella's vision for Al in pharma is both bold and grounded:
Speed up drug development so life-saving treatments get to patients sooner.
Boost diagnostic accuracy through continuous data and smarter pattern recognition.
Make care safer with predictive tools that catch dangerous drug interactions before they happen.
Move healthcare from reactive to proactive-intervening before symptoms even show up.
This isn't some far-off vision. It's already happening. The question isn't whether Al is ready; it's whether the industry is ready to keep up.
"If Al can generate Hollywood movies, why can't it generate life-saving molecules?"
Fail faster, heal smarter: How AI is rewiring pharma

Key takeaways
Failure isn't a dead end; it's a valuable learning tool. With Al, pharma can study what went wrong and use that insight to do better next time.
$ Investing in AI and digital health should be just as important as funding new drug research. Both are critical to the future of medicine.
AI-powered sensors and real-time diagnostics allow catching health issues early, shifting care from reactive fixes to proactive prevention.
To stay ahead, the pharma industry needs to embed AI throughout the entire development process, from discovering new molecules to delivering personalized treatments.

Chapter 3 Building trust at scale: LLMOps for safe, compliant AI in healthcare
Building trust at scale: LLMOps for safe, compliant AI in healthcare
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute

Artificial intelligence holds enormous potential for healthcare, but deploying it in environments governed by HIPAA, HITRUST, and NIST is like building a rocket in a minefield. One wrong move and sensitive patient data, clinician trust, or regulatory compliance could be at risk.

For Jeff McDonald, who leads both technology and security at Vituity, that challenge came into sharp focus during a routine internal review. A physician had already used a generative AI tool for documentation safely and effectively, but outside any formal policy framework. It was a wake-up call that revealed a widespread industry gap: while the appetite for AI grew fast, the guardrails weren't there.

Rather than clamp down, McDonald leaned in. By applying the principles of LLMOps, a structured approach to managing large language models across their entire lifecycle, he helped transform a shadow project into a secure, compliant, and scalable AI documentation platform.

Infrastructure such as code, automated compliance enforcement, multi-agent architectures, and human-in-the-loop design became the building blocks of a system that didn't just satisfy regulators; it used those standards as a foundation for innovation.

This chapter explores how Vituity makes generative AI safe for the emergency room. From reducing documentation time by hours per shift to embedding compliance deep in the dev pipeline, McDonald's approach reframes regulation not as red tape, but as a competitive advantage. And with hallucination-aware design and domain-specific agents, it points toward a future where AI becomes a reliable partner in care, not a liability.

Vituity's model offers more than a technical success story. It shows how large language models, when handled with the right mix of caution and ambition, can transform frontline medicine without compromising what matters most: safety, trust, and care.
Building trust at scale: LLMOps for safe, compliant AI in healthcare
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
1 The problem: AI in a tightly regulated healthcare world

During a routine internal review, McDonald came across a Vituity physician using an LLM to help with clinical documentation. The setup was technically compliant; it de-identified data and kept a human in the loop, but it highlighted a bigger issue. There was no clear policy or framework for using generative AI in clinical care.

This lack of structure isn't unique to Vituity; it's common across healthcare. And yet, the need for Al is real. Emergency physicians, for example, only spend about 25% of their shift actually treating patients. Another 25% goes to documentation and admin work.
One physician had even built their own Al tool to help with charting, but it wasn't scalable, and it didn't fully meet enterprise compliance standards.
The big question is: how do you safely scale AI in one of the most tightly regulated industries out there?
The compliance requirements are very demanding:
HIPAA demands strict safeguards for patient data
* HITRUST includes over 650 security controls
* NIST 800-53 sets the bar for federal-level cybersecurity

And on top of that, you've got the unpredictability of LLMs. They can hallucinate, carry hidden biases, and introduce security risks. Without clear oversight and guardrails, generative AI can end up doing more harm than good in clinical settings.
HIPAA
HITRUST
NIST 800-53
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
2 The solution: Building a secure LLMOps pipeline

Instead of shutting down the physician's homegrown Al tool, McDonald saw an opportunity. With oversight of engineering, app development, and product teams, he proposed a better path: take what worked and rebuild it at scale, securely, professionally, and fully compliant. That vision became the foundation for an AI documentation platform powered by LLMOps.

The key? Infrastructure as Code (IaC). Rather than setting up servers manually, every piece of infrastructure was written in code. That meant environments were consistent, version-controlled, and had compliance baked in from day one, not patched in after the fact.
V It reduced human error by avoiding an ad-hoc setup.
It boosted security through automated, standardized deployments.
> It sped up development by making testing and rollback quick and easy.

Automation was central to everything. Traditional compliance relies on manual checks and audits. McDonald's approach made compliance part of the build process. Every safeguard, encryption, access logging, and session timeouts, was coded into the system from the start.
Encryption and logging were enforced automatically.
Session timeouts and access controls came standard.
Continuous monitoring replaced the outdated, once-in-a-while audits.
As McDonald put it:
"We don't just comply with regulations; we use them to make our Al system stronger."

HIPAA, HITRUST, and NIST weren't seen as roadblocks; they became the blueprint. By working within these frameworks, the team built an AI environment that was more secure, scalable, and future-proof than anything they could've created outside of them.
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
3 Making AI safe for healthcare: The role of LLMOps

McDonald and his team built a complete LLMOps framework, an evolution of DevOps and MLOps to bring stability to the unpredictable world of generative AI. Still, they were designed specifically for large language models in clinical settings.
This wasn't a plug-and-play solution. It was built around five specialized pipelines, each designed to handle a different part of the system:
Development pipeline. Borrowing from traditional software development, this pipeline applied strict testing and quality checks-not just to code, but to prompts, data flows, and AI outputs.
V Infrastructure pipeline. Managed entirely through Infrastructure as Code (IaC), this ensured cloud environments could be quickly deployed, version-controlled, and closely monitored.
> Administration pipeline. Before the AI touched any real patient data, it was stress-tested in a sandbox. This let the team evaluate outputs and catch issues early.
Execution pipeline. Once live, the system stayed under close watch. Human-in-the-loop oversight meant that any strange or risky behavior could be caught and fixed in real time.
R&D pipeline. This is where the system learned and improved. Real-world feedback was used to retrain and refine the models, without ever compromising compliance or performance.
"Large language models are wild and unpredictable. But with the right LLMOps framework, they can be tamed for enterprise healthcare."
5 specialized pipelines
Development pipeline
Infrastructure pipeline
Administration pipeline
Execution pipeline
R&D pipeline
Building trust at scale: LLMOps for safe, compliant AI in healthcare
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
4 AI-powered physician documentation: A real-world use case

One of the biggest stress points in healthcare is documentation, especially for emergency room doctors, who are constantly juggling notes, forms, and records, often with little time and zero margin for error.

That's where Vituity brought its Al system to life. They built an LLM-powered documentation tool grounded in LLMOps, designed specifically for speed, security, and physician support. It runs in a fully secure, HIPAA-compliant environment and follows a "physician-first" approach:
V LLMs help draft clinical notes, turning free-text input into structured documentation.
Doctors stay in full control, reviewing, editing, and approving everything before it's finalized.
Sensitive data stays protected, with complete audit trails and compliance built in.
The results were immediate.

Doctors started saving two to three hours per shift, time they could now spend with patients instead of paperwork. The quality and consistency of documentation improved, which helped internal workflows and made audits smoother.
And because the system was built with compliance from the ground up, there were no security trade-offs, just better outcomes and less burnout.
Streamlined workflow
E
V
Input
Draft
Review
Finalized
Building trust at scale: LLMOps for safe, compliant AI in healthcare
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
5 The challenge of AI hallucinations and trust in LLMs

Even with the project's success, McDonald didn't lose sight of the risks. Large language models have a well-known flaw: they can produce confident, polished responses that are just plain wrong, a problem known as hallucination.

To keep things safe, the team set clear boundaries. The Al wasn't allowed to diagnose or suggest treatments. Its job focused on summarizing and formatting charts, areas where it could add value without entering dangerous territory.

McDonald also introduced a multi-agent setup. Instead of relying on one big, catch-all model, the system used multiple smaller agents, each focused on a specific task. One might handle clinical notes, and another would check for compliance. This approach made the results more accurate and, more importantly, easier to trace.
And the human-in-the-loop model stayed in place the whole time. No AI-generated content ever made it into a patient's chart without a doctor reviewing it first.

These guardrails weren't just about safety; they were about trust. The system earned confidence without compromising care by limiting AI to the right tasks, specializing its functions, and keeping physicians in control.

Compliance check
Clinical summary validation

6 Impact on healthcare

McDonald and his team pulled off something rare in healthcare IT by building AI into a secure, well- structured system. They created a tool that's both powerful and safe. The platform helped reduce physician burnout, sped up clinical workflows, and stayed fully compliant with all regulatory standards. Just as important, it laid out a path others can follow. The LLMOps framework, rooted in automation, strong governance, and clinical oversight, can be adapted by hospitals and health systems everywhere. It proves that with the right approach, Al doesn't have to be a risk. It can be a real asset.
Time saved: "2-3 hours per shift"

7 Next steps and future vision

Al in healthcare isn't just another enterprise tool; it has to be held to a higher standard. That means strict governance, constant monitoring, and rock-solid accountability. But with the right foundation, built on LLMOps principles, AI can be a game-changer.
As Jeff McDonald puts it,
"Regulations don't slow us down. They force us to build Al systems that are safer, stronger, and more resilient."
Looking ahead, McDonald and his team plan to:
V Roll out AI documentation tools to more departments and hospitals.
Evolve multi-agent AI systems to boost accuracy and reduce hallucinations.
Expand compliance automation to make regulatory tasks faster and easier.
The vision is clear: Al that's innovative and built to last in the real world of healthcare.
Milestones
More hospitals

Safe, small, and specialized: The future of pediatric AI

Paediatric healthcare is rich in data, but much of it remains locked away in unstructured formats, buried in free-text MRI reports, pathology results, and consultation notes. Despite the potential for life-saving insights, this complexity, combined with the scarcity and specificity of paediatric cases, makes it difficult for traditional AI systems to deliver meaningful results.

At Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH), this challenge is amplified by clinician burnout and documentation overload, where doctors routinely work overtime to keep pace with administrative demands. Yet applying Al isn't as simple as plugging in a commercial model; most are trained on adult data and falter in the nuanced world of child healthcare.
In this chapter, Pavithra Rajendran and Shiren Patel explore how GOSH is reshaping this landscape using small, fine-tuned language models (SLMs) that are purpose-built for paediatric care.

Through its DRIVE innovation unit, the hospital has developed a suite of LLM-powered pipelines that extract key insights from unstructured data, assist with documentation, and maintain clinician oversight at every step.
From contextual name extraction in MRI reports to real-time summarization of doctor-patient conversations, GOSH is turning the limitations of generic AI into a case for specialization.

By anchoring every tool in transparency, human validation, and ethical governance, GOSH isn't just using Al; it's redefining what responsible Al looks like in a paediatric context. The result is reduced clinician workload, faster patient communication, and models clinicians can trust.

As Al continues to evolve, GOSH's work serves as a blueprint for how healthcare innovation can remain deeply human, even when powered by machines. The future of paediatric medicine won't be built on off-the-shelf AI but on systems designed with children, clinicians, and context at their core.
Safe, small, and specialized: The future of pediatric AI

1 The problem: Unlocking paediatric healthcare data
Pediatric healthcare is packed with valuable data, but most of it goes untapped. At GOSH, every child's journey generates a wealth of information: appointments, scans, lab results, and surgeries.
However, a lot of this data is unstructured. MRI results show up as free text, pathology reports are filled with dense medical jargon, and consultation notes vary wildly depending on who writes them.

That inconsistency makes it hard to extract insights. On top of that, most AI models, especially ones trained on adult data, don't perform well in pediatric settings. The datasets are smaller, less uniform, and often involve rare conditions uncommon in broader populations. So, general-purpose models usually fall short when applied at a place like GOSH.

Clinician burnout only adds to the problem. Doctors can spend hours just documenting cases, often after hours. It's time-consuming, draining, and takes time away from patient care. It also slows down communication with families.
GOSH set out to solve this challenge with LLMs: unlocking the value of pediatric data, reducing the burden on clinicians, and doing it all without compromising patient safety.

2 The solution: Building LLM-based data pipelines
To tackle these challenges, GOSH built a set of LLM-powered pipelines with three main goals in mind:
Pull insights from unstructured medical texts, such as MRI reports and pathology results.
/ Lighten the documentation load for clinicians with Al-assisted drafting and summaries.
Keep patient safety and fairness front and center through strong human oversight and contextual checks.
These initiatives are led by GOSH's DRIVE innovation unit, which develops AI tools while making sure they meet strict clinical, ethical, and regulatory standards.

A big part of GOSH's approach is knowing that one-size-fits-all Al won't cut it. Instead of using large, general-purpose models, they rely on small, fine-tuned language models (SLMs) built specifically for pediatric care. These models are easier to control, simpler to validate, and better suited for environments where trust, accuracy, and safety are critical.

3 LLM use cases in paediatric healthcare
Use case 1:
Pulling context-based info from MRI reports

One of the first big challenges was digging through more than ten years of cardiac MRI reports to pull patient identifiers. Rule-based systems didn't cut it. Report formats varied, names were inconsistent, and key metadata was often missing, making rigid pattern-matching useless.

GOSH built a lightweight LLM trained to identify names using context clues to solve it. With smart prompt design and cues like titles, departments, and section headers, the model learned to tell the difference between patient names and clinician names, with impressive accuracy.

They used saliency heat maps to validate the model's decisions, showing exactly which parts of the text it focused on. That made it easier to confirm the model was doing the right thing and safe to deploy in a real-world setting.
Use case 2: Summarizing complex histopathology reports

Histopathology reports are among the most complex documents in healthcare: dense, technical, and challenging for non-specialists to understand quickly. GOSH needed a way to extract the key diagnosis fast, without sacrificing accuracy.
They trained a compact LLM (TinyLlama Chat) to do just that. The model generates a one-sentence summary of each report's main finding, giving clinicians a helpful starting point for review.

But they didn't just trust the output blindly. The team used natural language inference models to check the summaries for consistency and accuracy. With the right context, the model reliably captured the main diagnosis with minimal hallucination.
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai

3 LLM use cases in paediatric healthcare
Use case 3:
Automating clinical documentation with ambient AI

The most transformative use case was ambient AI. Like many hospitals, GOSH faces an ongoing challenge: doctors work long hours to finish notes and letters. The goal was to cut down that burden by capturing real-time clinical conversations and generating drafts automatically.

Now, the system records consultations and uses an LLM to produce structured summaries and patient letters. Clinicians still review and approve everything before it's finalized, keeping clinical control in place.

The impact has been enormous: less time spent on documentation, faster patient communication, and better work-life balance for clinicians. Looking ahead, GOSH plans to expand the system to handle things like lab test orders directly from transcripts, pushing automation even further.

5 Next steps and future vision

Great Ormond Street Hospital's work with LLMs sets a new standard for how Al should be used in pediatric care. By building AI tools that are tailored, trustworthy, and grounded in clinician oversight, GOSH isn't just pushing the tech forward; they're showing what responsible, ethical Al in healthcare really looks like.
AI in pediatrics has to be designed with care, context, and caution. Gains in speed and accuracy are valuable, but only if they lead to better outcomes for children.
This isn't about dropping generic Al into a hospital setting. It's about recognizing that pediatric data is different, as are the ethical responsibilities that come with it.

Chapter 5 From model to patient: How UHG builds trustworthy AI
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute

In healthcare, the promise of artificial intelligence is immense, but so is the risk of getting it wrong. AI can perpetuate bias, compromise safety, and erode patient trust when deployed without oversight. For Jason Haupt and the UnitedHealth Group (UHG) team, the challenge isn't just building powerful models; it's ensuring they're fair, ethical, and medically sound before reaching a patient.

UHG created a Machine Learning Review Board to meet this challenge; a rare blend of legal, clinical, and technical experts charged with reviewing every AI model in production. By the end of its first year, the board had reviewed over 500 models, with 97% requiring changes before deployment. But far from being a roadblock, this governance model has become a strategic asset, guiding teams to safer, more innovative implementations.

This chapter explores how UHG integrates AI across its vast healthcare ecosystem, from voice bots that help patients refill life-saving medications, to natural language search that makes finding the right doctor easier, to AI assistants that help clinicians and call center reps work more efficiently. Each tool is built on real-world testing, rigorous evaluation, and a deep commitment to human-centered care.

At UnitedHealth Group, Al doesn't replace healthcare professionals; it empowers them. Whether supporting nurses in the field with real-time decision tools or giving doctors more time with patients by automating administrative tasks, UHG's approach shows that operational Al, when done responsibly, can drive real outcomes at scale.

As Al continues to evolve, Haupt's work is a powerful reminder: innovation must walk hand in hand with integrity in healthcare. When governance becomes a guide, not a gatekeeper, AI transforms from a technical experiment into a deeply human solution.
From model to patient: How UHG builds trustworthy AI

1 The problem: Making AI work responsibly in healthcare

UnitedHealth Group runs one of the most complex healthcare systems in the world, and with that scale comes serious responsibility. When it comes to using Al, it's not just about building smarter models. It's about making sure those models are safe, ethical, and fair for everyone.

In 2022, UHG made that commitment official by launching a Machine Learning Review Board. The board brings together legal experts, compliance leaders, medical doctors, and machine learning professionals, all with equal say. Their job is to review every AI model before it goes into production. By the end of that year, they'd reviewed more than 500.

The process is challenging for a reason. Around 97% of models are sent back for changes, sometimes minor tweaks, occasionally complete overhauls. The board takes a "mitigation-first" approach, focusing on risks like bias and fairness before anything gets deployed.
For example, if a national dataset doesn't represent Latino patients in Texas, the model must undergo extra testing to ensure it works equitably.

And while the board is thorough, it's not there to block progress. As Haupt puts it, governance can either be the troll under the bridge or the Sherpa that helps you reach the summit. At UHG, it's definitely the Sherpa.

2 The solution: Real-world AI applications across UHG

The review board is just one piece of UHG's broader Al strategy. Haupt shared several examples of how Al is used daily to support patients, clinicians, and operations, with a strong focus on human-centered design.
Voice bots that help with medication adherence

One simple but powerful use case is AI voice bots that remind patients to refill critical medications. The bot asks a yes-or-no question, and the patient confirms if they need a refill. It may sound basic, but it solves a big problem: people missing meds due to forgetfulness or everyday challenges.

Of course, there are hurdles. For example, the system can have trouble understanding patients who speak English as a second language. To make sure no one is left behind, the Al includes a smart "off-ramp"; if the bot isn't confident in the response, it immediately transfers the call to a human rep. That balance keeps the experience accessible while still using automation effectively.
Smarter provider search

Finding the right doctor can be overwhelming. Most patients don't know what specialist they need or how to describe their symptoms in medical language. UHG's solution is a natural language Al search. Now, patients can just type in something like "stomach pain" and get matched with the right providers.
The tool powers tens of millions of searches and offers cost estimates, helping patients understand what they'll owe before they get care.
AI support for call center teams
UHG handles more than 300 million calls a year, so supporting call center reps is crucial. To help, AI tools transcribe calls in real time, surface helpful info, and auto-generate summaries.

On average, these summaries save 20-30 seconds per call, which really adds up. But more importantly, they let staff focus on the people they're helping instead of on documentation. That means faster support, less burnout, and better job satisfaction.
Real-time clinical support at the point of care

Take Lisa, a home health nurse visiting a Medicare patient named Ruth. Before the visit, Lisa gets an Al-generated risk profile based on Ruth's lab results, meds, and history. During the visit, Lisa adds that Ruth uses nicotine, and the AI instantly updates her diabetes risk score.
That real-time insight helps Lisa make better decisions on the spot. After the visit, the Al also creates a summary to reduce Lisa's admin burden and keep the whole care team in the loop.
These real-world tools show how AI, when done right, can reduce friction, improve outcomes, and support, not replace, the humans at the center of care.

4 Next steps and future vision

At its best, Al isn't here to replace doctors or nurses. It's here to help them do what they do best: care for people. At UnitedHealth Group, Jason Haupt and his team are proving what it looks like to make healthcare smarter. By weaving AI into core systems while keeping human needs front and center, they're building a future where care is more responsive, equitable, and effective.
AI might start with code, but its impact is personal. When designed thoughtfully and governed responsibly, it becomes a real force for good in healthcare.

Looking ahead, AI will play an even bigger role in both patient and provider experiences, not just at UnitedHealth Group, but across the industry. Expect more AI-driven touchpoints throughout the healthcare journey, from more precise billing explanations to better cost transparency. These tools will help patients navigate care with more confidence and less stress.

AI will keep working behind the scenes so that healthcare professionals can support their work, not replace it. Nurses, call center reps, and physicians will be equipped with intelligent tools that make their jobs easier and more impactful.
The result? More seamless, coordinated, and human-centered care.

But as AI continues to grow, leaders have to stay alert. These systems must be evaluated constantly, not just for performance, but for how well they align with both business goals and human needs. That's how we unlock Al's full potential in healthcare.

Chapter 6 The future of generative AI in healthcare
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook

The future of generative Al in healthcare
How far can LLMs go in clinical care?
Will AI ever make decisions on its own?
And what breakthroughs and guardrails do we need to move forward responsibly?
If there's one thing we've learned, it's that success in healthcare Al depends just as much on people as it does on the tech.
1 The biggest barriers are not technical

Most of the challenges in bringing Al into healthcare aren't about whether the models work; they're about regulation, trust, ethics, and making sure Al fits into how clinicians actually work. At UnitedHealth Group, every AI model goes through a formal review. Their Machine Learning Review Board evaluates each one before it goes live, as 97% require some kind of change.
"The most empowering part of my job is ensuring every Al model meets our ethical and medical standards before it reaches production," says Jason Haupt, Chief Data Scientist at Optum.
Josh Tamara Saver put it plainly: "Doctors don't reject Al because it can't think like them; they reject it when it behaves like it doesn't understand them."
Getting it right means introducing AI correctly by framing it properly, integrating it into workflows, and earning trust.
Compare technical vs. non-technical barriers:
Technical
20% (e.g., model accuracy, integration)
Non-technical (trust, ethics, workflow fit)

The future of generative Al in healthcare
2 Multi-agent AI and specialization over scale

Big general-purpose models may be impressive, but they often miss the mark in specialized areas like pediatrics or emergency medicine. In practice, smaller, more focused models tend to perform better.

At Great Ormond Street Hospital, for example, custom AI tools trained on pediatric data are already being used to summarize complex pathology reports and pull insights from over a decade of MRI scans.
"Pediatric healthcare data is unique. Al solutions built for adults don't always work," says Pavithra Rajendran.

Instead of relying on one big model, multi-agent systems are emerging as a smarter approach, breaking tasks into smaller pieces, each handled by a specialist agent. One model might handle documentation, another compliance, and another transcription.
Just like healthcare relies on teams of specialists, AI is evolving into a collaborative network of task-specific tools.

3 Will AI replace doctors?
Short answer: No.

Al will take over some of the busywork but won't replace clinical judgment. Its role is to support, not lead. The most effective AI tools act like junior doctors; they offer input, but the final call stays with the clinician.
Josh Tamara Saver warns,

"The real danger isn't Al making decisions, it's clinicians assuming they don't have to." That's why Vituity introduced the idea of the Al Resident: a supportive assistant that always defers to the doctor.
We're already seeing this in action. At Takeda and UHG, Al-powered documentation tools are helping providers reclaim hours without changing the clinician's role.
In one example, a home health nurse named Lisa uses real-time AI risk scores to guide her decisions. She's still in charge, just better equipped.
Generative AI in Healthcare eBook
aiai
Al Accelerator Institute
The future of generative Al in healthcare
4 Ethical dilemmas ahead
As AI gets more advanced, the ethical questions get more complex:
Who's responsible if Al-generated content leads to harm?
How do we validate fairness in training data in populations that are underrepresented?
What's the safeguard against confident hallucinations that could mislead care?
"Trust is earned in healthcare Al," said Shiren Patel of GOSH. "Every step we take must be validated by clinicians to ensure patient safety."

Transparency, ongoing monitoring, and full audit trails aren't optional; they're mandatory. At GOSH's DRIVE unit and Optum's Al review board, teams are already building governance playbooks to ensure safety and accountability.
The next big challenge? Scaling these frameworks without cutting corners.

Conclusion
Whether building lab-integrated sepsis predictors or AI residents that chart in the background, one principle holds true: the most successful AI is the kind that quietly helps, not loudly interrupts.

Real progress in generative Al won't come from technical breakthroughs alone. It will come from understanding people: how clinicians make decisions, what patients fear, and how healthcare really happens.
"Al can be revolutionary, but only if we apply it where it actually works. Don't let the hype distract from real progress," said Josh Tamara Saver.
That's the future worth building.
The future of AI in healthcare is human-centered. Systems must adapt to how clinicians work, not the reverse.
Regulatory, ethical, and trust-based barriers will decide adoption. Accuracy alone won't win over healthcare professionals.
Smaller, specialized, multi-agent systems will outperform large general models in clinical environments.
Al won't replace clinicians but will redefine how care is delivered. The transformation is already underway.
